evals.prompt: Test forms.html as if you were a Veteran filling out the form. Check that it looks right, works correctly, and is accessible. File: /sessions/eager-nice-euler/mnt/Aquia Form Builder/forms.html
evals.expected_output: A humanized test report covering visual appearance, functional flow, and accessibility (508/WCAG) with a pass/conditional pass/fail verdict
expectations: Report evaluates visual appearance (layout, colors, typography)
Report traces the user journey (intro → form pages → review → confirmation)
Report checks Section 508 / WCAG 2.1 AA compliance
Report checks screen reader compatibility (ARIA, labels, heading hierarchy)
Report includes a test verdict (pass/conditional pass/fail)
Report explains issues in user-impact terms, not just code terms

evals.prompt: Check if this form is accessible for someone using a screen reader. I need to make sure it's 508 compliant before we publish. File: /sessions/eager-nice-euler/mnt/Aquia Form Builder/forms.html
evals.expected_output: Focused accessibility audit covering screen reader support, keyboard navigation, ARIA attributes, contrast, and WCAG compliance
expectations: Report focuses primarily on accessibility rather than visual design
Report checks heading hierarchy for screen readers
Report checks form field label associations
Report checks ARIA attributes on dynamic elements (accordions, alerts)
Report references specific WCAG criteria by number

evals.prompt: The green start button and the arrow characters on the Continue/Back buttons look weird to me. Can you test whether those are correct? File: /sessions/eager-nice-euler/mnt/Aquia Form Builder/forms.html
evals.expected_output: Targeted analysis of the action link and button arrow components, confirming they match BIO patterns and explaining why they look the way they do
expectations: Report confirms green action link IS correct per BIO VaLinkAction pattern
Report confirms U+203A on Continue button IS correct per BIO pattern
Report confirms U+2039 on Back button IS correct per BIO pattern
Report explains WHY these patterns are correct (references VA.gov source)
Report evaluates whether these elements work well from a user perspective despite being unfamiliar
